text: Four trocars are positioned in the left lateral quadrant: two for robotic arms, a 12mm camera trocar, and one assistant port, establishing access for the robotic hernia repair procedure.

Four trocars are positioned in the left lateral quadrant: two for robotic arms, a 12mm camera trocar, and one assistant port. The surgeon begins dissection using a monopolar hook to remove part of the hernia sac, leaving a small portion intact and transecting it at a high point.
The surgeon begins dissection using a monopolar hook to remove part of the hernia sac, leaving a small portion in place and transecting it at a high point.

The surgeon bends the bipolar instrument to achieve effective retraction without grasping tissue. Using the hook in a bottom-up fashion, the posterior dissection proceeds with simultaneous dissection and retraction. After dissecting the hernia sac, a second larger cavity containing bowel loops is revealed.

The surgeon bends the bipolar instrument to achieve effective retraction without grasping tissue. For the posterior dissection, the monopolar hook is used in a bottom-up fashion, facilitating simultaneous dissection and retraction.

Following the dissection of the hernia sac, the surgeon discovers a larger cavity containing bowel loops. Using round tip eight millimeter scissors, the surgeon carefully dissects near the small bowel, ensuring minimal energy spread to the surrounding serosa. The left hand gently opens the tissue plane, minimizing risk to the small bowel.
The surgeon uses round tip eight millimeter scissors for precise dissection near the small bowel, carefully opening the tissue plane with the left hand to prevent injury to the surrounding serosa.

The surgeon encounters a complete fusion between the small bowel and peritoneum, a common finding in patients with ventricular peritoneal shunts, highlighting the potential dangers of dissection even with an open approach.

The surgeon encounters a complete fusion between the small bowel and peritoneum, a common finding in patients with ventricular peritoneal shunts. The dissection process is visually challenging and requires careful manipulation to avoid injury.
The surgeon utilizes a robotic platform to rotate a thirty-degree camera ninety degrees, enhancing exposure of challenging planes during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon utilizes a robotic platform to rotate a thirty-degree camera ninety degrees, enhancing exposure to challenging planes within the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the dissection, allowing the catheter to pass through the hernia defect. Small areas of deserosalization are repaired using interrupted stitches of PDS-IV-O. A careful inspection reveals no deeper injuries.

The surgeon completes the dissection, allowing the catheter to pass through the defect. Small deserosalizations are repaired using interrupted stitches of PDS-IV-O, with no deeper injuries observed upon careful inspection.
The surgeon approximates the two edges of the hernia defect using a short running suture with a size one V-lock barbed suture, effectively closing the defect under tension.
The surgeon approximates the two edges of the hernia defect using a short running suture with a size one V-lock barbed suture, effectively closing the defect under tension.
The surgeon carefully avoids applying excessive force to prevent obstruction of the small catheter, leaving an open passage that will be covered by mesh.

The surgeon prepares a 20x25 cm ventral light mesh with a longitudinal opening for the catheter. Temporary stay sutures are placed at the four quadrants of the mesh using straight needles to facilitate its placement, ensuring an open passage for the catheter without obstruction.

A 20x25 cm ventral light mesh with a longitudinal opening for the catheter is prepared. Temporary stay sutures are placed at the four quadrants of the mesh using straight needles to facilitate its placement.
A small opening is created around the catheter to prevent obstruction, followed by the closure of the mesh around the catheter using Proline-II-O sutures.
The surgeon creates a small opening around the catheter to prevent obstruction, then uses Proline-II-O sutures to close the mesh around the catheter, ensuring secure placement.
The surgeon employs a parachute technique to secure the mesh in one corner, compensating for the inability to place a stay suture, similar to techniques used for vascular structures.

The surgeon places short, 2-0 Prolene running sutures around the mesh to close the longitudinal opening, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned over the hernia site.

The surgeon places short, 2-0 Proline running sutures around the mesh to close the longitudinal opening. The lateral quadrant is sutured with ease, utilizing a technique that involves a 180-degree rotation of the fingers on the control for complete needle rotation.
The surgeon sutures the lateral quadrant of the mesh, using a technique that involves a 180-degree rotation of the fingers on the needle control to achieve complete needle rotation.
